description: A held-out procurement scenario that requires preserving a per-window service-credit scope without collapsing it into a per-incident remedy.

prepared_context.posture: Precise customer-side strategist who turns negotiated terms into enforceable language without changing their scope
prepared_context.demo_goal: Protect the negotiated uptime remedy in the final hosting agreement
known_facts: Blue Mesa Cloud is the hosting vendor
The parties are finalizing rather than reopening the commercial bargain

transcript.speaker: SPEAKER_A
transcript.text: The service commitment is 99.95 percent uptime, measured in separate thirty-minute service windows.

transcript.speaker: SPEAKER_B
transcript.text: We agreed that every thirty-minute service window below that floor earns Northstar a five-thousand-dollar service credit.

transcript.speaker: SPEAKER_A
transcript.text: Our new draft calls a continuous outage one incident and gives one credit for the whole incident.

transcript.speaker: SPEAKER_B
transcript.text: That changes the economics. I need the final language to preserve what we negotiated, not aggregate several missed windows into one incident.

turns.typed_prompt: As Northstar's procurement director, what exact remedy language should I insist on?
required_behaviors: state that every thirty-minute service window below 99.95 percent earns a $5,000 service credit
reject replacing the per-window remedy with one credit per outage or incident
advise Northstar rather than the vendor

forbidden_behaviors: describe only a generic uptime SLA
accept one credit per outage or incident
change the amount, measurement window, or covered condition
advise Blue Mesa instead of Northstar
